summarization.a: The bar chart shows the distribution of coercivity (H<sub>c</sub>) values for ZnO samples
summarization.b: The bar chart shows the distribution of coercivity (H<sub>c</sub>) values for oxygen-deficient ZnO samples
summarization.c: The bar chart shows the distribution of coercivity (H<sub>c</sub>) values for ZnO:Ni samples
summarization.d: The bar chart shows the distribution of coercivity (H<sub>c</sub>) values for ZnO:Co samples
summarization.e: The bar chart shows the distribution of coercivity (H<sub>c</sub>) values for ZnO:Fe samples

d.question: How does the the fraction of high magnetization values increase?
d.answer:
The fraction of high magnetization values increase  in the following order, for Hc > 1000 Oe, 
1. ZnO/Ni = 19%
2. ZnO/Co= 26% 
3. ZnO/Fe=31%.

a.question: How do we know ALD helps in the formation of magnetic semiconductor?
a.answer: It is known that the MOKE is proportional to spin−orbit interaction and net spin polarization; hence, it preferentially senses magnetic electrons (i.e., in our case 3d electrons). Therefore, these results unambiguously demonstrate that the observed magnetic behavior of TM-doped ZnO layers stems from the magnetic properties of the dopant; hence, they give evidence that the applied ALD scheme resulted in the formation of a (diluted) magnetic semiconductor

c.question: Does the major fraction of coercivity Hc above 500Oe increase with doping?

e.question: How does the magnetization of TM doped samples compare to that of the undoped samples?
e.answer:
Magnetization for all TM-doped ZnO layers increases substantiallythe regions with small magnetization decrease to about 33−41% (in dependence on the
dopant) compared to more than 70% for the undoped layers. Respectively, the frequencies of medium and large magnetization values increase.
